@torch.compile(mode="max-autotune", fullgraph=True)
def _attn_backward_compiled(dO, P, P_dropped, V, mask, p_drop):
    """
    Fused attention backward exploiting GQA structure.
    Uses bf16 matmul for speed (V not expanded to 80 heads),
    promotes to f32 only for softmax backward computation.
    dO: [bs, 80, sq, 128] bfloat16
    P, P_dropped: [bs, 8...

current_paradigm.idea: Flash-attention-style fused Triton kernel using CUDA graphs + optimized memory layout
current_paradigm.description: Implement two highly optimized Triton kernels: (1) dS kernel that processes [bs*80, sq, skv] with each program handling one row of sq, loading P and P_dropped tiles along skv, computing dP=dP_dropped*mask/scale, then softmax_bwd = P*(dP - sum(dP*P)) all in registers without materializing dP_dropped globally - instead computing it on-the-fly from dO and V using tl.dot. (2) dV kernel that uses GQA structure: each Triton program handles one (bs, kv_head, skv_block) and accumulates over all 10 query groups and sq positions. Wrap with torch.cuda.make_graphed_callables or use cudagraph_trees via torch.compile with CUDA graphs to eliminate kernel launch overhead. The fused approach for dS avoids writing dP_dropped to HBM entirely - dO @ V^T is computed tile-by-tile inside the kernel and immediately used for softmax backward.
current_paradigm.what_to_optimize: Eliminate HBM writes of intermediate dP_dropped tensor, reduce kernel launch overhead with CUDA graphs, maximize arithmetic intensity
current_paradigm.cautions: The fully fused dS kernel (computing dO@V^T on-the-fly) requires loading both dO and V tiles which increases register pressure. May need to tune BLOCK_SIZE carefully. For non-square cases (sq != skv), need proper boundary masking. The GQA dV kernel needs to handle the group reduction correctly.

active_paradigms.idea: Custom Triton kernel fusing dropout backward + softmax backward + both BMMs
Flash-attention-style fused Triton kernel using CUDA graphs + optimized memory layout

import torch

NUM_ATTENTION_HEADS = 80
NUM_KEY_VALUE_HEADS = 8
HEAD_DIM = 128
NUM_GROUPS = NUM_ATTENTION_HEADS // NUM_KEY_VALUE_HEADS  # 10


@torch.compile(mode="max-autotune", fullgraph=True)
def _attn_backward_compiled(dO, P, P_dropped, V, mask, p_drop):
    """
    Fused attention backward exploiting GQA structure.
    Uses bf16 matmul for speed (V not expanded to 80 heads),
    promotes to f32 only for softmax backward computation.
    dO: [bs, 80, sq, 128] bfloat16
    P, P_dropped: [bs, 8...

current_paradigm.idea: Custom Triton kernel fusing dropout backward + softmax backward + both BMMs
current_paradigm.description: Write a Triton kernel that processes tiles of the attention weight matrix. For dS computation: load tiles of P and dP_dropped, apply dropout mask and scale, compute softmax backward locally. For dV: accumulate contributions from P_dropped^T @ dO across the sq dimension. The key insight is that the [bs, 80, sq, skv] tensors are the bottleneck - reading them multiple times is expensive. A fused kernel reads P, P_dropped, dropout_mask once and computes both dS and partial dV in one pass. Use separate Triton kernels for dS (fused with dropout) and dV (exploiting GQA by having each kernel block handle one KV head, accumulating over 10 query heads). For dV specifically: launch [bs, 8, skv//BLOCK, 128//BLOCK] blocks, each iterating over sq and the 10 groups, accumulating in fp32 registers. For dS: launch [bs, 80, sq, 1] blocks, each computing the row-wise softmax backward.
current_paradigm.what_to_optimize: Memory bandwidth - avoid multiple passes over large tensors, reduce global memory traffic by fusing elementwise ops with matmuls
current_paradigm.cautions: Need careful handling of non-power-of-2 sequence lengths with masking. The GQA structure means dV kernel should accumulate over 10 groups. Use tl.dot for the matmul tiles. Ensure bf16 inputs with fp32 accumulation. Handle the bs*80 batch dimension carefully for parallelism.

active_paradigms.idea: Custom Triton kernel fusing dropout backward + softmax backward + both BMMs
Flash-attention-style fused Triton kernel using CUDA graphs + optimized memory layout

import torch

NUM_ATTENTION_HEADS = 80
NUM_KEY_VALUE_HEADS = 8
HEAD_DIM = 128
NUM_GROUPS = NUM_ATTENTION_HEADS // NUM_KEY_VALUE_HEADS  # 10


@torch.compile(mode="max-autotune", fullgraph=True)
def _attn_backward_compiled(dO, P, P_dropped, V, mask, p_drop):
    """
    Fused attention backward exploiting GQA structure.
    Uses bf16 matmul for speed (V not expanded to 80 heads),
    promotes to f32 only for softmax backward computation.
    dO: [bs, 80, sq, 128] bfloat16
    P, P_dropped: [bs, 8...

current_paradigm.idea: Pure PyTorch with careful memory layout optimization: avoid torch.compile overhead, use einsum/bmm with optimal tensor layouts and explicit float32 promotion only where needed
current_paradigm.description: Skip torch.compile entirely (it adds JIT overhead on first calls and may not optimize well for variable shapes). Instead: (1) Use contiguous bf16 tensors throughout, only promoting to float32 for the softmax backward numerics. (2) For dP computation, use torch.baddbmm or reshape+bmm exploiting GQA - reshape dO to [bs*8, 10, sq, 128], V to [bs*8, 1, 128, skv], use expand+bmm to get [bs*8, 10, sq, skv]. (3) For softmax backward, keep in float32 but use in-place operations to reduce memory allocation. (4) For dV, use einsum('bgsq,bgsqd->bgsd', P_dropped_grouped, dO_grouped) which PyTorch can optimize to a batched matmul with summation. (5) Use torch.cuda.Stream to potentially overlap dS and dV computations. Avoid .contiguous() calls where possible by ensuring tensors are already in the right layout.
current_paradigm.what_to_optimize: Reduce Python/compilation overhead, minimize memory allocations, use optimal BLAS calls for the specific GQA structure with bs*8 independent matmul batches
current_paradigm.cautions: Without torch.compile, need to manually ensure tensor contiguity for efficient matmul. The expand() for V broadcasting must be followed by contiguous() before bmm or use as_strided carefully. Stream parallelism between dS and dV paths requires careful synchronization. Test that einsum maps to efficient cuBLAS calls rather than slow element-wise ops.

Flash-attention-style fused Triton kernel using CUDA graphs + optimized memory layout

current_paradigm.idea: Custom Triton kernel fusing dP computation, dropout backward, and softmax backward into a single kernel, with a separate optimized dV kernel exploiting GQA
current_paradigm.description: Write a Triton kernel that: (1) for dS computation - loads dO tiles and V tiles (shared across 10 query heads per KV head), computes dP_dropped = dO @ V^T in the kernel, applies dropout mask and scale, then computes softmax backward dS = P*(dP - sum(dP*P)) all in one fused pass without materializing dP; (2) for dV computation - write a separate Triton kernel that accumulates contributions from all 10 query groups into dV, loading P_dropped and dO tiles. Use block sizes tuned for B200 (BLOCK_M=64, BLOCK_N=64, BLOCK_K=128). The key optimization is that V tiles are reused across all 10 query heads in the same KV group, reducing memory bandwidth by 10x for the V loads. Store dS directly in bf16. Use tl.dot for tensor cores.
current_paradigm.what_to_optimize: Memory bandwidth reduction by fusing operations and exploiting GQA structure to reuse V across 10 query heads, eliminating intermediate tensor materialization of dP_dropped [bs,80,sq,skv]
current_paradigm.cautions: Triton kernel correctness is tricky - test against reference carefully. Handle non-power-of-2 sequence lengths with masking. The softmax backward requires accumulating sum(dP*P) which needs a reduction within the kernel. Use float32 accumulators for the matmul and softmax backward. Be careful with the GQA indexing: head_idx // 10 gives the KV head index.

import torch

NUM_ATTENTION_HEADS = 80
NUM_KEY_VALUE_HEADS = 8
HEAD_DIM = 128
NUM_GROUPS = NUM_ATTENTION_HEADS // NUM_KEY_VALUE_HEADS  # 10


@torch.compile(mode="max-autotune", fullgraph=True)
def _attn_backward_compiled(dO, P, P_dropped, V, mask, p_drop):
    """
    Fused attention backward exploiting GQA structure.
    Uses bf16 matmul for speed (V not expanded to 80 heads),
    promotes to f32 only for softmax backward computation.
    dO: [bs, 80, sq, 128] bfloat16
    P, P_dropped: [bs, 8...

current_paradigm.idea: Inline CUDA kernel using load_inline with cuBLAS batched GEMM and custom elementwise CUDA kernels for the fused dropout+softmax backward
current_paradigm.description: Use torch.utils.cpp_extension.load_inline to write a CUDA C++ extension. Implement two parts: (1) A custom CUDA kernel that fuses the dropout backward (mask*scale) + softmax backward (P*(dP - sum(dP*P))) in a single pass over the [bs,80,sq,skv] tensors using shared memory for the row sum reduction. (2) Use cublasGemmStridedBatchedEx for the two BMMs with the GQA structure - for BMM1 call it bs*8 times with stride tricks to handle the 10 groups, for BMM2 similarly. Use CUBLAS_COMPUTE_16F for bfloat16 operations. The fused elementwise kernel should process rows in parallel with one warp per row for the reduction.
current_paradigm.what_to_optimize: Minimize memory bandwidth by fusing elementwise operations, use hardware-optimal GEMM for the matmuls, avoid intermediate tensor allocations
current_paradigm.cautions: cuBLAS bfloat16 support requires sm_80+, B200 is sm_100 so this is fine. Need to handle the GQA striding carefully - the 10 groups share the same V matrix. The softmax backward row-sum reduction needs careful warp-level reduction. Must handle variable seq lengths across the 16 benchmark cases without recompilation.

current_paradigm.idea: Pure optimized PyTorch without torch.compile, using optimal tensor layouts and einsum for GQA-aware computation
current_paradigm.description: Remove torch.compile entirely to avoid compilation overhead and JIT variability. Use torch.einsum for the GQA-structured BMMs which allows PyTorch to pick optimal CUDA kernels. For BMM1: use einsum 'bghqd,bksd->bghqs' where g=8,h=10. For BMM2: use einsum 'bghqs,bghqd->bgsd' then sum over h. Keep all operations in bfloat16 except the float32 softmax backward. Use .contiguous() strategically to ensure optimal memory layout before large matmuls. Pre-compute the scale factor. Use torch.addcmul or fused ops where possible for the softmax backward.
current_paradigm.what_to_optimize: Eliminate compilation overhead, reduce memory allocations, use optimal BLAS kernels for the specific GQA shapes
current_paradigm.cautions: Ensure contiguous memory layout before matmuls. The einsum approach may or may not be faster than explicit reshape+matmul - test both. Avoid unnecessary float32 promotions. The dropout mask application and softmax backward should be fused if possible using a single pass.

active_paradigms.idea: Pure optimized PyTorch without torch.compile, using optimal tensor layouts and einsum for GQA-aware computation
Inline CUDA kernel using load_inline with cuBLAS batched GEMM and custom elementwise CUDA kernels for the fused dropout+softmax backward

current_paradigm.idea: Inline CUDA kernel using load_inline with cuBLAS batched GEMM and custom elementwise CUDA kernels for the fused dropout+softmax backward
current_paradigm.description: Use torch.utils.cpp_extension.load_inline to write a CUDA C++ extension. Implement two parts: (1) A custom CUDA kernel that fuses the dropout backward (mask*scale) + softmax backward (P*(dP - sum(dP*P))) in a single pass over the [bs,80,sq,skv] tensors using shared memory for the row sum reduction. (2) Use cublasGemmStridedBatchedEx for the two BMMs with the GQA structure - for BMM1 call it bs*8 times with stride tricks to handle the 10 groups, for BMM2 similarly. Use CUBLAS_COMPUTE_16F for bfloat16 operations. The fused elementwise kernel should process rows in parallel with one warp per row for the reduction.
current_paradigm.what_to_optimize: Minimize memory bandwidth by fusing elementwise operations, use hardware-optimal GEMM for the matmuls, avoid intermediate tensor allocations
current_paradigm.cautions: cuBLAS bfloat16 support requires sm_80+, B200 is sm_100 so this is fine. Need to handle the GQA striding carefully - the 10 groups share the same V matrix. The softmax backward row-sum reduction needs careful warp-level reduction. Must handle variable seq lengths across the 16 benchmark cases without recompilation.
